sources.notes: Statement and hard-version constraints with very large total n.
Official editorial confirms combining the reflection formula with divisor-style coefficient aggregation.
Luogu solution used to cross-check the hard-version summation optimization.

annotation.summary: Count the same palindrome-related binary strings under much larger bounds.
annotation.constraints: n can be extremely large across tests, so the E1 direct summation must be reorganized with number-theoretic preprocessing.
annotation.core_idea: Keep the reflection-principle/binomial formula from E1, then group terms by divisibility structure so repeated coefficients can be accumulated with a divisor sieve or linear-sieve style preprocessing. This turns the combinatorial expression into a fast aggregated summation.
annotation.complexity: Near-linear preprocessing over the maximum n plus fast per-test aggregation
tricks: Reuse the reflection-principle formula from the easy version.
Use sieve-style accumulation to handle the larger constraints.

solution_variants.name: reflection-formula-with-divisor-sieve
solution_variants.summary: Derive the reflection/binomial formula and aggregate its hard-version coefficients using divisor-sieve preprocessing.
solution_variants.complexity: O(N log N) preprocessing or better depending on sieve implementation

tags.evidence: The hard version still starts from the boundary-walk reflection formula for the counted strings.
tags.description: Count lattice paths or prefix-walk sequences with boundary constraints by reflecting invalid paths across a boundary.
tags.created_reason: Reflection-principle counting is a recurring combinatorics technique not captured by generic combinatorics or DP tags.

tags.evidence: The hard constraints require aggregating formula terms by divisor structure with sieve-style preprocessing.
The optimized expression is built from identities over binomial coefficients.
The problem is solved by deriving and evaluating a combinatorial counting formula.
The grouping criterion in the fast summation is divisibility of indices.
DP is a possible viewpoint for small constraints, but the accepted hard solution is formula plus sieve.
